content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Pirate Movies that are not pirates of the caribbean (which is goated to be fair)

**Score:** 55
**Body:** Pirate movies scratch a weird itch that others outside the topic don't quite hit for me. Vikings are similar enough I guess but the whole vibe is wrong. Pirates of the Carribean while not a cinematic masterpiece is fire enough, but wondering if I've somehow overlooked some elite pirate movies you all might be aware of?

  
Any thoughts?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Our Flag Means Death is fun.
2. Muppets Treasure Island
3. Look up the filmography of Errol Flynn and start with Captain Blood. The last good one is Against All Flags. 

Others but not Flynn's include The Black Swan, The Crimson Pirate,  The Prince of Pirates.

Robert Newton played Long John Silver in Treasure Island. He was also Blackbeard. Arggghh!

Edit: The Crimson Pirate
4. Not a movie, but Black Sails is really good
5. How did no one else say Master and Commander?
6. Ice Pirates
7. Treasure planet!
8. The Pirate Movie from the 80s. It’s a musical. 
9. Yellowbeard
10. Captain Philips 

## 2. Movies set entirely on trains?

**Score:** 35
**Body:** Just the other day watched a movie called Transsiberian, was a good thriller. Wondering what other good movies there are that are set on trains? Any genre

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Snowpiercer
  - Talk about "set on a train."
2. Train To Busan (horror, zombie).
3. Bullet Train
4. Source Code

It's not entirely set on a train but most of it and it's good!
5. Silver Bullet

Snowpiercer

Murder on the Orient Express

The Darjeeling Limited

Train to Busan

Under Siege 2

Runaway Train
6. The Taking of Pelham 123
7. Unstoppable is breathtaking,
8. **The General (1926)** \- silent action comedy

**Murder on the Orient Express (1974)** \- murder mystery
9. The lady vanishes 
The murder on oriental express

## 3. Movies about women focusing on themselves, being “selfish”, and/or figuring out life

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Am not feeling like a baddie and am currently feeling lost and also in the mood to watch a baddie switch gears from focusing on others to focusing on herself - any sort of iteration of a woman’s “coming-of-age”. I’ve seen Frances Ha and Worst Person in the World and loved them (see my last post here for context lol), but am looking for a movie with less time realizing they’re lost and more time finding themselves. Whatever that means to you! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “Wild” with Reese Witherspoon might fit! Based on a true story / memoir of the same title!
2. Brittany Runs a Marathon (2019)
3. Only Yesterday 
4. Fried Green Tomatoes (1991)

This is a little abstract to what you’re asking but Frances McDormand in Fargo always gives me a huge boost as a well written solid female character.
5. Shirley Valentine 
6. These are more about finding themselves but they mostly end positively and are worth a watch:

Amelie 

Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore 

Smithereens

Ladybird

Mistress America 

Girlfriends 
7. First Wive's Club 

It follows the paths of three best friends who are wildly different from each other. They each "focus on themselves" to different results but amazing for each of them. 
8. Young Adult


Best Sellers
9. Under the Tuscan Sun
10. How Stella Got Her Groove Back

## 4. Watch Dinner In America if you haven’t!!

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Great original cult classic. If you have a library card or attend college it’s free on a few streaming apps linked to public libraries.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Loved it!  They filmed the pet store scenes at the store I worked at and I got to sit about 15 feet away and watch them film-it was awesome!
2. I FREAKING LOVE this movie, and it made me a total Kyle Gallner fan girl.
3. Snack Shack by the same director was also great!
  - Hell yeah, I love that movie 
4. Probably might even find it on Internet archive 
5. Was such a pain for me to find...rented from Youtube year, year and half ago, and finally bought the blu-ray for my wife for Xmas. Def one of our fav films to watch together!
  - Was on Hulu for a long time. Really deserves more attention 
6. Such a fun movie
7. Definitely needs more love.
8. I just watched it I loved it. It’s on Kanopy - a free streaming service with no ads and you sign up to it with a library card. Tons of other movies which are impossible to find on regular streaming services. 

If I had to describe dinner in America I’d say it’s a wholesome Natural Born Killers 

## 5. I need life-changing dark or funny British , Irish or Scottish movies

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I’ve seen almost all of Mike Leigh’s  work. Four lions, kes, Nil by mouth , dead man shoes, this is England.

I’m trying to find anything that has meant something to someone or you think. Best represents the British, Scottish and Irish film industry.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 'Withnail & I' (1987)
2. The Guard

In Bruges

Life of Brian
3. Give Lynne Ramsay and Danny Boyle a try:


Shallow Grave


Morvern Callar


Trainspotting


Ratcatcher 
4. A Fish Called Wanda (1988) - Charles Crichton  
Snatch (2000) - Guy Ritchie  
Trainspotting (1996) & Millions (2004) both from Danny Boyle  
The Commitments (1991) - Alan Parker  
Life of Brian (1979) - Monty Pythons  
Sightseers (2012) - Ben Wheatley  



5. Local Hero

Waking Ned Devine

Death at a Funeral

The Death of Stalin
  - Local Hero is fantastic
6. In Bruges (2008)

Banshees of Inisherin (2022)

Small Things Like These (2024)

That They May Face The Rising Sun (2023) is more meditative slow but beautiful Irish movie 

Also not a movie but might be worth checking out this series- Such Brave Girls (2024) !!!! 
7. Trainspotting
8. Banshees of Inisherin
9. The Coronetto Trilogy, especially Hot Fuzz and Shawn of the Dead

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. The versatile Gary Oldman turns 68 today

**Score:** 2765

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He's an incredible actor! Lots of respect for him!
  - Bring me everyone. EVERYONE!

![gif](giphy|sUNqplVFtsctW|downsized)
  - So a Oldman is getting old, man he is good actor
2. The Goat as far as i am concerned.
  - He's got that rare ability to completely disappear into any role. Absolute legend.
3. Clearly having the time of his life in Slow Horses
4. ![gif](giphy|MAukeVX7xEwPAGFJsh)

Sexy as ever!
5. Happy birthday Gary!

From; EVERYONE!!
6. Dressed as a Victorian gentleman in Dracula 1992, he took my breath away. The most charming and urbane monster - a talent of his, I think.
7. https://preview.redd.it/f1zy007mpdqg1.jpeg?width=495&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=2e50a57e638c7f224e62760da10c0c119cab6798

## 2. What's a movie where an actor was so far out of his depth you don't even understand why they took the script?

**Score:** 1462
**Body:** I knew it was a joke for Ashton Kutcher to think he could play Steve Jobs so I never bothered to watch the movie.  I came out to my living room today and my wife was asleep with the movie Jobs playing on the tv.  Didn't take but a minute to realize how terrible Ashton was.  It was almost like he was playing a caricature of Jobs.  He just doesn't have the talent for that kind of role.

So this made me wonder what other movies out there are there where the lead had no business taking on that role.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Every character Ashton Kutcher plays always looks and sounds like Ashton Kutcher. 
  - "DUDE!"
"SWEET!"
    - DUDE WHERES MY MAC?
2. You remember that one James Bond film, where they needed an actress to play a Nuclear Physicist.......and they went with Denise Richards?
  - “I thought Christmas only comes once a year”.


3. https://preview.redd.it/ehzny7m07bqg1.png?width=1650&format=png&auto=webp&s=72e147b86277b085ca18ce79b373b585e2159a59

Keanu Reeves In Dracula
  - I love this Dracula so much The One True Old Man always comes through.......also it seemed like Anthony Hopkins was having fun playing Helsing ......
  - The thing is, he kind of nails the characterization of Jonathan being awkward and out of his element. But I don’t think that was on purpose. And the accent is atrocious. 
4. I worked at a company that he had invested in. During the pandemic he joined an all-hands as, I don’t know, a perk? We were all sitting in our homes, many people having improvised a desk space in their bedroom and wondering if the world would run out of toilet paper. He joined from a leather couch underneath a Basquiat painting and told us about how as a startup we needed to buckle down and put in the long hours to grind away and succeed. 

I had over a decade in startups as a software engineer at that point. So I knew that there is more to it than long hours and grinding it out - even then, especially then.

And this asshat had the audacity to pretend that his experience playing Steve Jobs in a movie gave him the real-life experience to speak about what it’s like to do our jobs. Fuck that guy. 

5. I 100% understand why Kutcher took that role, he thought Steve Jobs was an inventor and he thought himself an artist. He was wrong on both counts and that’s why the movie was dogshit.

## 3. Ryan Gosling’s last decade has been one of the finest runs for any actor. Hit or flop aside, he has delivered some really good films with great consistency. What’s your favorite Ryan Gosling film from the last decade?

**Score:** 1075

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His performance in The Nice Guys is effortlessly incredible, there should be an entire trilogy with him and Russell Crowe as those characters. 
  - The Nice Guys is so underrated. Great movie! Blade Runner 2049 would be the my top pick.
  - That movie was so good I didn't want it to end. Wish we got a sequel.
  - The fact there isn't is a shame. I would have loved another one where Ryan and Russell tackle another hyper specific conspiracy that was historically accurate. 
2. Blade Runner 2049
3. I love all these movies but I find myself watching Fall Guy & Nice Guys most often.  But I mean... 2049 and Barbie are modern classics. But think about it...  the 10 year stretch before that included Blue Valentine, Place Beyond the Pines, Drive, and Lars & The Real Girl. What a career.
4. Drive

Edit: just realised it's 2011 so not really last decade
5. Also the papyrus sketches from SNL are dope 
6. I still enjoy watching Crazy Stupid Love
7. Blade Runner 2049.

I am looking forward to Hail Mary though

## 4. Why do people like Anora so much?

**Score:** 972
**Body:** Can someone explain to me why so many people think that this movie is a masterpiece? I myself don’t understand why Anora is given so much credit and how it won so many Oscars. Mikey was good, but not amazing and the plot wasn’t interesting for me. Maybe the film editing is good. 

Can anyone please explain Sean Baker’s genius to me? 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think the main thing is how it switches genres and tone so effectively throughout. Plus like you said it's very well edited. Brisk. Mikey's performance never falters either. Best picture was a surprise but I could understand her Oscar.
  - Yes, made me laugh, angry, sad and cry all in the same movie. Absolutely loved going through the range of emotions.
2. It’s risqué style is not for everyone, I get that. I found it breached a nice balance between privilege, trauma and identity. 

Mikey Madison shows a lot of range too. I don’t think that’s an easy performance to give.  
  - Especially when you see interviews of her and she’s this eloquent, soft-spoken person. Her performance was fantastic.
    - I do think this is one of those times a performance is enhanced and almost completely carried by seeing the actor's persona irl. She is so convincingly Anora, I was shocked when I saw interviews with her and she was so quiet and mild mannered. I thought the movie was fine, but her performance really is a tour de force
3. Good movie. Realistic vversion of Pretty Woman. The guy did such a great job playing an entitled, no direction child of a millionaire. I like after watching it. One of the very few movies where there were no likeable characters.
  - Agree with everything you said, although I thought the bodyguard who got her ring back at the end was likable. Can't remember his name. He was hilarious though.
    - Igor and Anora were so likeable imo
  - Igor was very likeable to me
4. I really enjoyed this film. I found its heightened tone and the escalating search/chase to be very funny. It's a caper that launches an emotional sneak attack on you if you stay with it.

The "core" of the humour, for me, was dramatic irony. 

We as the audience know that Ani's relationship with Zak is a sham. We can see it plainly, and Ani's insistence on ensuring she gets paid shows that deep down she knows it too. However, she's just clueless/hopeful enough to behave as though it might not be. That this man-child might be her prince. That's darkly funny.

Likewise Toris and Garnik are fools. Classic, theatre-esque Shakespearean idiots. They're incapable and patently ridiculous but present themselves as tough, resourceful gangsters. Honestly I found that hilarious in the vein of the UK office.

Then the sneak attack... Igor. He's really the audience surrogate here, and won over almost immediately by this spirited, whirlwind of a woman. He knows his bosses are idiots and enjoys watching her make a fool of them. He's saddened to watch her chase the dream she'll never have. And at the end, when she loses it all, he offers her something she didn't even realise she wanted. Something she'd told herself doesn't exist.

So... That's my take, that's the film I watched. The comparison to Shakespeare I think is the most apt. It's a silly, hilarious farce that smuggles a tender love story under our noses.

## 5. We need more movies like this. That's it. That's my whole review of this movie.

**Score:** 431

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cinematography from Grieg Fraser is stunning and worth the ticket alone. 
2. The kind of movie that made me go through a spectrum of emotions and made me want to do it all over again. 👎 fist all my bumps 
3. Amaze. Amaze. You watch. I sleep.
  - Amaze amaze amaze!
    - Amaze amaze amaze!
4. Here's hoping because Lord and Miller also optioned We Are Legion, We Are Bob last year for either a film or series.  If you haven't read that book, it's a solid sci-fi good time.
5. If this doesn’t win at minimum best visual effects we all riot
  - The character of rocky was so well realized it’s insane! Then there’s all the shots of him overlooking that planet (not going to spoil the name)
  - Visual Effects

Set Design

Cinematography

Direction

Best Actor
6. It was SO DAMN GOOD

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Public humiliation and familial shame in Uncut Gems

**Score:** 53
**Body:** Uncut Gems is oddly one of my big comfort movies.

I just rewatched last night and I noticed a couple themes that I may have missed on my last rewatch.

Howard is a single minded hustler in pursuit of the next big win or thrill of the gamble while trying to turn his uncut gem into a big financial win and reach personal transcendence as it’s clear his personal identity is tied to the gem as he explains to KG.

One thing I realized upon my third rewatch is that after each time Howard is publicly humiliated there is a sort of familial whiplash or fallout that derides from his actions. Howard is stripped by Arno’s goons and tossed into a trunk where his wife finds him. Then we move to a conversation Howard has with his daughter where she barely says anything to him but we can clearly see she’s ashamed of him. After, Howard is embarrassed and thrown out after trying to confront The Weeknd. He has a public meltdown and berates Julia telling her he’s done with her and leaves her in tears on the street.

Then shortly after we see Howard slap a smoothie spilling it on her face in front of the apartment tenants and he tells them in the elevator she’s crazy.  And then even when his own son questions him about the girl his neighbor said lives in his apartment he chastises him telling him to shut up. For some reason this reminded me of the conversation Howard has with his father in law in the first act. After they have Passover dinner Howard’s father in law tells him that having Arno in his home is like a stranger in his home mentioning how Arno had wished a merry Christmas in the past.

These scenes kind of seem redundant on the surface but I feel in conjunction with the scene of Howard at his daughter’s play where he talks with another family about their vacation plans and he says he has none. The other family asks him why and he says because his kids are getting stupid. The man replies his kids will then be stupid in Cancun. The look on Howard and his wife face shows they took that hard. Then after this Arno arrives with his goons and all serve to underline how his actions cause a spiral effect of negative consequences which cause him to go back those same behaviors.

I’ve always made the thematic connection in this film with Howard’s dogged pursuit of the next win both a matter of self actualization for him as he doubles down on it after every degradation, but obviously digging his own grave with the addiction.

I wonder then, what’s the thematic insight the Safdies were trying to impart with these scenes of familial shaming in Howard’s relationship with Arno and Julia/ family or what they’re saying in regards to the way we behave/react under public humiliation.

Hopefully this made some kinda sense not an expert

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t know of I’m completely off with this but …

Prior to Marty Supreme, I didn’t think all that much about the Jewishness of the Safdies. For example, one of the brothers named Broadway Danny Rose on their Sight and Sound top ten. It’s a Woody Allen film with a mix of farce, caper, and subculture tourism in the world of Broadway talent agents. The fact that it’s a chaotic story of a neurotic ordinary schmo finding himself in uncomfortably close quarters with the mafia makes it seem like an obvious Safdie precursor — you don’t even have to think that its subculture, NYC comic talent agents, is a Jewish thing, and diamond sellers in Manhattan is a Jewish thing.

I’ve only watched Uncut Gems once, but I found it significant that, already towards the start, hard battle lines are drawn between Arno and Howard. This is established, but shortly after that they’re forced to shuffle around one another in a kind of brief truce at a relative’s house for Sabbath. This makes the conflict between seem all the more intimate and particular.

Arno’s desire for Howard’s public humiliation is an aspect of this weird dynamic. Arno obviously feels that humiliating Howard publicly is a particularly appropriate component of his punishment. That says something about the emotion he feels towards Howard — what is the emotion? Disgust at the type of man Howard is?

It seems clear that the Safdies have written this scenario in order to explore a very particular type of psychology. After Marty Supreme, it’s clear that they want to look at their characters’ quirky psychologies as phenomena of the Jewish character, or the NYC immigrant Jewish neighborhood character, or the peculiar diamond district subculture character.

In other words, I would say that to understand the behavior in Uncut Gems, maybe it would be helpful to apply the lens that, like Marty Supreme, the film is an instance of Jews writing penetratingly and hyper-specifically about Jews.

I recently read another example of this genre: Neal Gabler’s An Empire of their Own: How the Jews invented Hollywood.

Here’s some snippets from that on the psychology of the gambling that the moguls participated in:

>women were really a distraction that became a metaphor for power. The Hollywood Jews had another vice—a metaphor that became an obsession. They loved to gamble. 

>Gambling was a kind of therapy that cut
through all the affectations and reduced everything to basic naked aggression. It tested the skills of bluff and nerve and judgment, which were the measure of a film executive, and it provided a small arena for tremendously competitive and insecure men to intimidate one another and prove themselves. 

>At the same time it provided anxiety, which, writer Leo Rosten observed, “is provoked, nursed, and kept alive in a manner which suggests self-punishment for obscure and disturbing guilts” —possibly, though Rosten made no conjecture, the Jews’ guilt for having rejected themselves or possibly guilt over their sense of unworthiness in the face of success. And, finally, it provided a way to cheat destiny by mastering it. For the Jews, most of whom felt they had been matched against destiny, the last may have been the most important.



  - Oh certainly this is essential. I saw this movie with my Jewish father, and after it was over it took him hours to relax because of how much Howard reminded him of his own father. (The other strongest cinematic parallel is Royal Tenenbaum, if that helps paint the picture.) The man in question basically cratered a very successful family business with one very bad bet on the stock market. He spent his kids' college funds in secret. He went to court over tax fraud after my dad refused to bail him out again. I never knew my grandmother and my grandfather died when I was fairly young, but by all accounts he was chaotic - he could be enormous fun (this I experienced), but also the sort of person you had to follow behind lest he let a heavy door close on his grandkid because he simply wasn't paying attention. 

While I think the Safdies are doing more than simply recording the fact, the fact is that these men had tremendously chaotic effects on their families, very much including humiliation (without even beginning to get into the distaste for Jewish women many of them had, which is also evident in Howard). My dad's family's entire class status changed almost overnight, and while they were not impoverished, it came with a thousand small humiliations from their perspective. To cover the madcap neurosis without that side is dishonest.
  - I think you gave a very insightful comment here that really helped me contextualize the film better. When you mention that in Rosten’s book how there were a socioeconomic sub culture of Jews that “may have guilt over their sense of unworthiness in the face of success” really hits the nail on the head. It recontextualizes why Howard cares about betting and the gem so much as it’s his own way of proving his worthiness and value. 

I think once again you really made a deep insight when you mention how Arno may feel “disgust at the type of man Howard is.” Because like I said earlier, Arno is not a Jew and in fact his own father in law has showed grievances to Howard about it saying he doesn’t know what it feels like because Arno isn’t married to his own daughter.

I think there’s enough here to reasonably believe that Arno feels a sense of spite at the kind of person Howard represents. Before the goons stripped Howard, Arno screams at him for “resurfacing his swimming pool” and asks him over and over how does he think that makes him look. 

This makes me wonder how Arno himself made his money, and why he let Howard borrow that much? This is an even more interesting family dynamic than I thought, it’s more nuanced than it seems. Crazy you got this much on one watch lol.
2. Marty Supreme was pretty heavy on the humiliation too. >!Like him getting caught in Central Park, him burning a bridge with the friend who let him stay at his house, him doing embarrassing skits with the Harlem Globetrotters...and of course the spanking scene!<
3. Nice analysis. Yes , interesting how the goons tried to use public humiliation to turn him around.  That’s a hard movie for me to be a comfort film but enjoy. I guess The Hateful Eight is and it’s also very edgy. 
4. I don't really get what you're trying to say- it's a lot of examples without making the larger point- but I do agree that he seems to go through a shaming ritual in the whole film. The idea being, which I wonder why you didn't connect the dots for us more, is that he's willing to embarrass himself and his family, so dogged is he in pursuit of his gambling addiction/next win.

## 2. Relay | Good Spy-like Thriller

**Score:** 17
**Body:** *Relay*, starring Riz Ahmed and Lily James, is about a guy who helps clients negotiate a safe exit from powerful people. It’s directed by David Mackenzie who did films like *Hell or High Water* and *Outlaw King*.

It’s not a spy film but feels like one with surveillance and counter surveillance, secret drop offs and, people in grey vans tracking out protagonist who is in turn tracking them.

Riz Ahmed and Lily James are really good in this. I called the twist right from the beginning but the film managed to make me doubt myself just long enough to catch me off guard. I liked it because it ended up addressing a frustration I was having with a particular character. Suddenly it made sense.

It’s one of those films you could describe as robust. There’s not a lot of fluff in the writing. Characters get just enough characterisation. Relationships get mist enough depth. Where that approach fails somewhat is that this story could have used some directorial flair. Think how *Munich’s* espionage sequences are directed.

Maybe there’s a Michael Mann-esque version of this film that’s slicker and full of vibes. The AA meetings and backstory are stripped bare. Imagine a *Thief* like soundtrack (yeah I know a lot of people didn’t care for its music). I think that might have been a better telling of this story but what we got is still a lot of fun.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Honestly had a serious suspension of disbelief problem for me, was just hard for me to get over the central gimmick:


Where they constantly just have these super secret insider info/ spy dealings relayed through these minimum wage call center employees. I am usually not a nitpicker but that just pulled me viscerally out of the movie every time they did it.


Without that I think I might have also considered it a really good thriller. Nice twists and turns otherwise. But yeah, that central element somehow took me out of it, and made me watch it pretty disengaged.
  - > Where they constantly just have these super secret insider info/ spy dealings relayed through these minimum wage call center employees. I am usually not a nitpicker but that just pulled me viscerally out of the movie every time they did it.

I don't understand. The relay callers are picked at random, there's a record of what they say, the situation unfolds in real time for them, and it would be risking their jobs and freedoms to get into something they can only see fragmentarily. What could they do? All collude? I thought about it during the movie but I couldn't come up with anything.
    - >it would be risking their jobs and freedoms to get into something they can only see fragmentarily


Yeah, but to me it just felt very viscerally nonsensical. You dont just dictate your top secret info to some random civilians in detail. Over and over.


>What could they do?


Idk, tell the cops, the fbi, cia, the press, anybody? I mean the point is they are talking *exactly* about the most incriminating details on that service. And over and over again. At just every single point every single one of these guys could be some guy who is like "damn I need to tell this to someone" and runs straight to the authorities and is "hey, I got some info, corporation x is doing XYZ, check that out".


They wouldnt even have to incriminate themselves, they could just send it as an anonymous letter, it could still fuck up the corporation badly and easily.
      - > Idk, tell the cops, the fbi, cia, the press, anybody?

Under the rules that they function in the US, the contents of the call are strictly confidential.

They don't have access to people's names and locations. Even if they did share their suspicions (which they could and probably would get fired for doing), they don't have much to go on and the communications are not acceptable as evidence nor basis of an ongoing investigation.

So, no, the movie is pretty believable in how relay call services work. You can check it out here:

https://transition.fcc.gov/Bureaus/Common_Carrier/FAQ/faq_trs.html
        - I mean, I know thats not the way to go with movies like that, but can you imagine people dealing with that kind of stuff, utilizing the relay like that in real life?


Its ridicolous! Yes, there are these smart ass arguments like you just did, but in practice every time you dictate your super secrets the hair on the back of your neck would stand up, and for good reason!


You are just building your whole super spy organisation around this glaring source of insecurity.


Like yeah, there are *some* arguments why you might get through with it some or most of the time, but every single time you are playing dice with some 1 billion dollar corporate lawsuit.


Its their established method. Every case the whistleblower might just tell you something rather specific, and every single time the call center rando might just decide to tell somebody.


There are other way to go about this. Letters, meet in the park, middle men, what have you. They are even constantly showing smart phones.  Lots of ways to be *pretty* secure on the internet.





Also from your own link:


>


>How can I be assured my TRS calls will remain confidential?




>**Except as authorized by section 705 of the Communications Act, 47 U.S.C. 605**, CAs are prohibited from disclosing the content of any relayed conversation regardless of content and from keeping records of the content of any conversation beyond the duration of a call, even if to do so would be inconsistent with state or local law. CAs are prohibited from intentionally altering a relayed conversation and, unless federal, state, or local law prohibits (**for example, use of phone for illegal purposes**), must relay all conversation verbatim unless the relay user specifically requests summarization.



So yeah, proper legal backstops are there. Its set up so the employees dont yap about your embarrassing fetish shit or whatever, not the state setting up some hotline thats like "yes, please, do your mafia shit over this service and we cant ever do anything about it, hahaha"

## 3. Close (2022) - childhood, friendship, societal expectations and grief

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Close was a beautiful, devastating coming-of-age film about two close boys and the breakdown in the relationship as they reach their teens. One becoming more aware of societal expectations of the behavior of boys and the other unaware or unaffected by it and retaining the sensitivity of expression and tenderness.

But the second half of the film became one about grief and guilt. This becomes the focus of the film, but I felt the suicide undercut what could have been a great film had it focused the trajectory of the whole story on the ending of a close friendship, continuing the theme of societal expectations of behavior from childhood to adulthood. We're immediately caught up in this relationship but then it forces us to focus on sudden tragedy. Although it handles it so well that you're still gripped in a feeling of loss and sadness even as it shifts that sadness from the loss of a friendship to a different kind of sadness through the loss of life. The film really handles the character of Leo very well. It's realistic in that we see him in moments of happiness around other kids and new friendships and instances of healing, and as he's caught off guard with intercepting moments of loss and memory.

The suicide of Remi felt heavy-handed and unnecessary. Again, because it shifts the source of the feeling of sadness that was already established by the hurt feelings involved when one child pushes the other away, even if it handles this shift well.  It is at least redeemed by not explicitly blaming the cause on Leo pushing Remi away, even as Leo blames himself. Rather, it takes a more important role serving as the source of guilt and grief, regardless of the actual cause. I think it was right to maintain some ambiguity surrounding the cause of the suicide.

One major misstep the film took was the car ride where Leo admits to having pushed Remi away and taking blame. Sophie tells him to get out of the car through a momentary moment of anger or blame, although it's clear Leo is not to blame for Remi's suicide, and I feel Sophie knows that.  Leo runs out of the car and Sophie runs after him. She finds him scared and crying and raising a branch at her. Was he planning to strike Sophie with it? It was a moment of violence or threat that just felt off with how the film had built their relationship. It felt out of scale, a tendency that felt too melodramatic for what this film was and how sensitively it handles human emotions up until then.

That being said, it was one of the best, most-engrossing films I've seen in a while. It felt like where French cinema was in the 90s. It also reminded me of The Stone Boy (1984), another film about childhood grief and expression.

The film is streaming free on Tubi right now.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I went into this film in the cinema without knowing anything about it, and it absolutely floored me. I know what you mean about Remi’s suicide - it’s very much a film of two halves. But I found myself so moved by the exploration of his family’s grief that I didn’t mind the tonal shift. Likewise, I understood at the end the theme was circling back to instinctive behaviour and what happens if we strip everything away at the end. I also thought the soundtrack was gorgeous. I still listen to it and think about the film. Thanks for this write-up.
2. I'm from Belgium and I watched this film in class (not film school) in two parts. It came out exactly right so that the first session we saw everything before the suicide, and everything after the suicide in the second session. My classmates (who, with all respect, don't know jackshit about storytelling or film) kept saying "yeah yeah I bet one of them is gonna die" and all of them agreed on it. I didn't think so but I stayed quiet. It just didn't make sense, I was lowkey frustrated that they even thought that because it wouldn't work story-wise. This film wouldn't in any way benefit from the death of one of the boys and I didn't for one second believe that was going to happen. 
But yeah, it did and it's still my biggest criticism of the film to date. Potential thrown away for shock factor. I'm glad to see someone else share this opinion
  - I agree and felt the same, especially for the shock factor part. Worse, right after the suicide, I couldn’t help but think “of course they killed Rémi over Léo, they kept putting Léo in the forefront and investing a lot less in Rémi’s story.” The more the movie went on the more I felt like it had used Rémi to furnish Léo’s character, so it could focus on him and his story and how he saw their relationship and the judgement of others, and how he’d be affected afterwards. It didn’t give Rémi enough of a chance, and I almost felt like he was being killed just so the directors could get the audience’s hearts wrenching and feeling for their movie. It felt instrumentalised to me, both to improve their true lead character Léo, and to win some sympathy from the audience who feel touched and saddened by the tragedy of such an and event.

 Part of it is of course intentional: suicide leaves so many questions that will never be answered, some without even hints of what the right answer may be. Giving too much away about Rémi gives us a possibility of building a narrative that really often isn’t fully there during a suicide. But I still think, as great as the first part was, that it ought to have shown a bit more of Rémi and a bit less of Léo, who already occupies so much of the second half anyway. When you’re close to someone who committed suicide suddenly like this, that’s the biggest heartbreak of it all anyway: you’re left overflowing with memories of them, both the mundane and the highlights, and you’re extremely aware that they used to be a person existing and living in the world, having influence on you as much as you had influence on them. You have to adjust, because so much of your mind is still presuming erroneously that they are still alive and going about in the world. It’s the default you’ve lived with for so long, and you projected yourself and them into the future thinking that this normal would stay permanently like this. Your brain likely did not even imagine the possibility of them doing this. Every time you encounter something you’d want to talk to them about you have to retrain yourself to think “oh wait, but they’re dead, and I have to get used to this.”
All of what you used to share with someone is now yours alone, and it’s strange and horrible to think about, because you don’t even get the reassurance that if you yourself were to disappear one day, at least they’d still be around to keep having an effect on the world. You feel like you’re one of the few things left proving they existed and had a mark on the world, and even that is slowly fading.

Anyway sorry for the rambling. I felt like the film utterly failed to capture any of that specifically because there wasn’t much of Rémi left to remember anyway. I mean, we see Léo go through it, but we don’t understand it ourselves. And what’s the point of movies if not to try and make you understand what his experience could be?
By the end of it (though I did like the ending) it almost felt like it was fetishising sadness just to get some awards. It didn’t truly understand youth suicides.

A movie that showed the reality of losing someone so suddenly that I found did it much better was Three Colours: Blue or Margaret (although that one is a stranger dying).
3. >*Was he planning to strike Sophie with it?*

I had always interpreted him raising the branch as him defending himself. He was worried *she* was going to attack *him*. 

He genuinely believes he's responsible for the loss of her son, and she reacted with anger to his initial confession. He's also only a little boy. It would be reasonable for him to think that the adult chasing him through the woods is coming to hurt him.

I think it's seeing that image - the scared little boy cowering in fear of her - that fully snaps her out of her anger.
